title: Email and Phishing Protection Policy
content:
Email and Phishing Protection Policy v1.8 — Aligned with NIST SP 800-177, CISA Phishing Guidance, and Cisco Email Security best practices.

1. EMAIL SECURITY CONTROLS
1.1 All inbound email must pass through the enterprise email security gateway with: anti-spam filtering, anti-malware scanning, URL rewriting and sandboxing, and attachment sandboxing.
1.2 Email authentication protocols must be enforced:
  a) SPF (Sender Policy Framework) — published for all enterprise domains with hard fail (-all)
  b) DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) — enabled for all outbound email with 2048-bit keys
  c) DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication) — enforced at p=reject for all enterprise domains
1.3 TLS must be enforced for email in transit. Opportunistic TLS is the minimum; mandatory TLS must be configured for known partner domains.
1.4 External email must be tagged with a visible [EXTERNAL] banner to alert recipients.

2. PHISHING AWARENESS AND REPORTING
2.1 All employees must complete phishing awareness training within 30 days of hire and annually thereafter.
2.2 The security team must conduct simulated phishing campaigns at least quarterly.
2.3 Employees who fail simulated phishing tests must complete additional targeted training within 7 days.
2.4 Click rates above 5% on simulated phishing campaigns must trigger organization-wide awareness communications.
2.5 Employees must report suspicious emails using the Report Phishing button in the email client. Do not forward suspicious emails to colleagues.
2.6 The SOC must triage reported phishing emails within 1 hour during business hours.

3. EMAIL USAGE RULES
3.1 Enterprise email must not be used for: personal business, mass unsolicited messaging, forwarding chain letters, or transmitting offensive content.
3.2 Auto-forwarding email to external addresses is prohibited unless approved by IT Security for documented business need.
3.3 Confidential and Restricted data must not be sent via email unless encrypted using enterprise-approved email encryption (S/MIME or message-level encryption).
3.4 Email retention follows the Data Retention Schedule. Emails older than 3 years are automatically archived. Emails older than 7 years are purged unless under legal hold.

4. BUSINESS EMAIL COMPROMISE (BEC) PREVENTION
4.1 Financial transaction requests received via email (wire transfers, payment changes, invoice modifications) must be verified through an out-of-band communication channel (phone call to a known number).
4.2 Requests to change vendor banking information must be verified directly with the vendor through established contacts.
4.3 Executive impersonation detection must be enabled in the email security gateway.
4.4 New domains resembling the enterprise domain (typosquatting) must be monitored and reported to the security team.

5. ATTACHMENT AND LINK SAFETY
5.1 Executable file types (.exe, .bat, .cmd, .ps1, .vbs, .js, .msi, .scr) must be blocked at the email gateway.
5.2 Macro-enabled Office documents (.docm, .xlsm) from external senders must be blocked or quarantined.
5.3 Compressed archives (.zip, .rar, .7z) from external senders must be scanned and sandboxed before delivery.
5.4 URLs in emails must be rewritten through the secure email gateway for click-time analysis.
5.5 Employees must hover over links to verify the destination URL before clicking. When in doubt, navigate to the website directly rather than clicking the email link.

6. REFERENCES
- NIST SP 800-177 Rev 1 Trustworthy Email
- CISA Phishing Infographic and Guidance
- Cisco Email Security Best Practices Guide
- CIS Controls v8 Control 9.6, 14.1-14.3
- DMARC.org Implementation Guide
